title: Shortbread rounds
subtitle: Classic three-ingredient Scottish shortbread
excerpt: Plain shortbread made with flour, butter, and caster sugar in the classic 3:2:1 ratio. Rolled to 8 mm and cut into rounds, baked pale at a low temperature, and cooled before lifting. Makes about twenty.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: shortbread is recorded in the biscuit chapter as a Scottish preparation made with flour, butter, and sugar in a firm short dough. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: shortbread biscuits made by the creaming method are documented with the note that the butter should be worked in thoroughly to produce the characteristic crumble. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the 3:2:1 flour-to-butter-to-sugar ratio is recorded as the standard Scottish domestic proportion.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled shortbread layered with baking paper in a tin for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Double easily — bake on two trays on the same shelf.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well in an airtight tin for 2 weeks. The flavour deepens over the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the shortbread should be pale gold, not brown — brown means over-baked

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough that contains a high proportion of fat to flour, producing a crumbly, tender texture with no chew. The fat coats the flour particles and inhibits gluten development; the result breaks cleanly rather than stretching. Shortbread, pastry, and sablé biscuits are all short doughs. The less water in the dough, the shorter the result — shortbread contains none.

content.text: — there is no liquid, and the butter is the only thing binding the flour and sugar together. The texture should crumble cleanly with no chew. Bake it pale; brown shortbread has lost its delicacy.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a large bowl until pale and smooth. Add the {{plain-flour}} and work it in with your hands until the mixture comes together into a smooth dough. Press it into a flat disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes; the chill firms the butter and makes the dough easier to roll without cracking.

Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to 8 mm thick; thinner than this and the rounds will be fragile and prone to over-browning. Cut rounds with a 5 cm cutter dipped in flour, pressing straight down without twisting. Space 3 cm apart on the trays. Prick each round twice with a fork to prevent the tops blistering. Bake for 20–22 minutes until pale gold across the top. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes to firm before transferring to a rack.
